text:
Recent achievements in solvent extraction hydrometallurgy obtained at the ICMM have enabled us to study in a comprehensive way the coordination chemistry governing the whole process of the commercial solvent extraction operations. It appears that, for the majority of commercially used systems applied to hydrometallurgy, it is possible to obtain the elementary acts contributing to the great efficiency and selectivity of the solvent extraction processes and to the metallic recoveries of various hydrometallurgical operations. The high demand for some strategic metals, the environmental advantage derived from the substitution of the pyrometallurgical processes by liquid-liquid extraction, make necessary to investigate the intimate metal transfer mechanism in order to establish new improved efficient and economic extraction processes for the purification of metals which constitute the basis of our technologies for the refineries of the future.

According to the metals transported into the organic phase, commercial extractants are generally classified into three categories: metal cations, metalates and metal salts. Taking a wide range of existing extractants, such as phenolic oximes, phosphorus(v) acids and tertiary amines, as examples, the relationship between inner-sphere coordination characters including structure characteristics of the ligands and the selectivity of the extracted metals, and the outer-sphere effects including ion-pair and hydrogen-bond interactions enhanced by the low dielectric constant of hydrocarbon diluents were examined in detail. By linking metallurgy and supramolecular chemistry, this paper aims to illustrate scientific approaches for the development of efficient extractants to meet the demand of efficient metallurgy for high grade metallic ores, which are becoming rare, and the efficient utilization of low grade metallic ores and metallic waste streams.
